The saguaro is a tree-like cactus species in the monotypic genus Carnegiea that can grow to be over 12 meters tall. It is native to the Sonoran Desert in Arizona, the Mexican state of Sonora, and the Whipple Mountains and Imperial County areas of California. As an Arizonian the saguaro is a regular sight everyday, but still beautiful when you actually stop and look.

Saguaro (Carnegiea gigantea) is a species of cactus endemic to the southwestern regions of Arizona, Mexico, and California. It can grow up to 40 feet in length. Saguaros also make good homes for a variety of bird species including woodpeckers and desert doves.

Family: Cactaceae 

Modified leaves, spines.

Endangered and protected species, floral dissection not possible.

The Saguaro, can grow substantially tall as seen in the pictures. The flower that blooms on top of the Saguaro is the Arizona state flower. They can also have no arms or up to 49 arms depending on the growth of the Saguaro. Their blossoming times are usually around the end of May, beginning of June. Due to the increased amount of time in the day, and warmer temperatures in the spring.

In this ecosystem, there are a few animals that feed on these plants. It can be difficult to survive in a really stressful environment without a constant supply of water. Cacti plants must find a way to adapt to their surroundings and live comfortably. To survive and reproduce, they must do everything they can. These plants have shallow roots that extend far from the plant, allowing them to absorb as much water as possible when it rains.

The saguaro is a tree-like cactus species in the monotypic genus Carnegiea that can grow to be over 12 meters tall. It is native to the Sonoran Desert in Arizona, the Mexican state of Sonora, and the Whipple Mountains and Imperial County areas of California. The saguaro blossom is the state wildflower of Arizona .
This is a Saguaro Cactus, scientific name Carnegiea gigantea. They are located in the Sonoran Desert.

This is the year of unusual blooming characteristics for the Saguaro cacti.  It seems that the plants that are in irrigated landscaping are not displaying this style of flower distribution.  Plants in the wild that are not growing out of cracked bedrock seem to have this "extra bloom" feature.  A result of drought-stress??

This area of Sonoran Desert, like that to the southwest around the Phoenix area, harbors an abundance of red brome in the understory. This is ecologically analogous to cheatgrass (Bromus tectorum) in the sagebrush steppe, where the abundance of an exotic annual grass in native vegetation has the ability to transform it after burning. Fortunately, not much of the vegetation in this regions appears to have experienced burns in recent history.

description: A staple to the desert, these provide homes for Woodpeckers and other birds.  They also have long gevity, and are natives to the desert around us.

towering, columnar form, often reaching heights of over 40 feet, with prominent, upward-curving arms that give them a distinctive, tree-like appearance. Their pleated, ribbed surfaces allow them to expand and store water efficiently, while their dense covering of sharp spines provides protection from herbivores.

distinguished by their tall, columnar shape and large, branching arms that curve upward, often giving them a human-like silhouette. Their ribbed surface allows for significant expansion and contraction to store water, and they are covered in protective spines, which are arranged in clusters along their ridges.

Spotted a saguaro cactus (Carnegiea gigantea) along the Telegraph Pass trail. It had several thick arms reaching upward, with a green, ribbed surface covered in sharp spines. The cactus was growing in rocky, dry area and was fully exposed to the sun. Surrounding vegetation included brittle bush and creosote, typical of the Sonoran Desert habitat. The saguaro appeared healthy, though not in bloom, and stood prominently against the desert landscape.

This slightly slanted saguaro cactus is in bloom and currently displays Arizonas state flower. The cactus was found on a warm (85°F) and windy morning in May. The iconic saguaro can be found throughout Arizona, and this particular one was located just off of the Sweetwater Preserve in Tucson, AZ. The soil today was slightly less dry than other mornings; this change is probably due to recent rainfall. The photographed cactus is about 7ft tall, has long vertical ribs, white flowers on the crown, and spines throughout. This cactus is probably relatively young because it has no arms and is not nearly maxed out in height (the saguaro can grow to 40ft!).

The saguaro cactus is a desert plant found in Arizona. They are known for their extreme lifespans, slow growth, and adaptation to all desert conditions. Saguaros are very important to the ecosystem, providing food, shelter, and nesting sites for many different animals.

Even though native shrubs like Krameria erecta are common at this site, this area of Sonoran Desert, like that to the southwest around the Phoenix area, harbors an abundance of red brome in the understory. This is ecologically analogous to cheatgrass (Bromus tectorum) in the sagebrush steppe, where the abundance of an exotic annual grass in native vegetation has the ability to transform it after burning. Fortunately, not much of the vegetation in this region appears to have experienced burns in recent history.

From FNA online: Krameria erecta produces fruits or capsules with stout spines, which usually bear curved multicellular hairs on the basal half and retrorse barbs near tip.

This site lies northeast of Phoenix, Maricopa County, Arizona, along highway 87 near Camp Creek and N Forest Road 68.
